    Interstellar Medium (ISM)

    Interstellar Medium

    Type:Foundational concept
    Composition:~99% gas, ~1% dust by mass
    Phases:Hot ionized, warm ionized/neutral, cold neutral, molecular
    Related: Molecular clouds
    Magnetic fields
    Turbulence

    The interstellar medium (ISM) is the matter and radiation that fills the space between stars within a galaxy. It consists of gas (mostly hydrogen and helium) and interstellar dust, threaded by magnetic fields and permeated by cosmic rays. The ISM is the raw material from which new stars form and into which dying stars return processed material — it is the ecosystem of galactic evolution.

    Phases of the ISM

    The ISM exists in several distinct thermal phases in approximate pressure equilibrium:

    • Hot ionized medium (HIM) — T ~ 10⁶ K, n ~ 0.003 cm⁻³; fills most of the volume, heated by supernova remnants
    • Warm ionized medium (WIM) — T ~ 8000 K, n ~ 0.3 cm⁻³; traced by Hα emission and pulsar dispersion measures
    • Warm neutral medium (WNM) — T ~ 6000–8000 K, n ~ 0.5 cm⁻³; traced by 21 cm H I emission
    • Cold neutral medium (CNM) — T ~ 50–100 K, n ~ 30 cm⁻³; traced by 21 cm H I absorption
    • Molecular clouds — T ~ 10–20 K, n ~ 10²–10⁶ cm⁻³; traced by CO and other molecular lines; sites of star formation

    Key Physical Processes

    Three forces dominate the dynamics of the ISM: self-gravity, magnetic fields, and turbulence. Their interplay determines whether gas collapses to form stars or remains supported against collapse. In dense molecular clouds, gravity eventually wins, but magnetic fields and turbulence regulate the rate and efficiency of star formation.

    The ISM is highly structured, with filamentary morphologies observed at all scales from parsecs to hundreds of parsecs[1]. These filaments are intimately connected to the magnetic field geometry — Planck observations show that B-fields tend to be perpendicular to dense filaments but parallel to diffuse striations[2][3].

    Observational Tracers

    • 21 cm H I line — traces atomic hydrogen in warm and cold neutral phases
    • CO rotational lines — primary tracer of molecular gas
    • Dust continuum emission — traces total gas column density at far-infrared/submm wavelengths
    • Dust polarimetry — maps magnetic field morphology via aligned grain emission
    • Faraday rotation — traces magnetic fields in ionized gas

    See Also

    • Molecular clouds — the densest ISM phase
    • Interstellar dust — the solid-phase component
    • Magnetic fields — threading and structuring the ISM
    • Turbulence — driving ISM dynamics
    • Star formation — the endpoint of ISM collapse
    • Infrared dark clouds — the densest, coldest ISM structures

    References
    1. Arzoumanian, D., et al. (2011). "Characterizing interstellar filaments with Herschel in IC 5146." A&A, 529, L6. DOI:10.1051/0004-6361/201116596
    2. Planck Collaboration (2016). "Planck results: Magnetic field structure of the ISM." A&A, 586, A138. DOI:10.1051/0004-6361/201525896
    3. Soler, J. D., et al. (2017). "B-field orientation transition in molecular clouds." A&A, 603, A64. DOI:10.1051/0004-6361/201731049
